entities.name: jñāna
entities.cite: When someone does not doubt a cognition — a seeing that is, as it were, his own possession

entities.name: pratyakṣa
entities.cite: it stands on the side of perception

entities.name: tarka
entities.cite: Argument gets its grip only where doubt has a foothold

entities.name: niśchaya
entities.cite: their cognition arrives with the self-evidence of perception

axioms.name: asrita
axioms.cite: The appeal to what stands pratyaksa-pakse, on perception's side, cuts in every direction

axioms.name: atyanta-nirvikalpa
axioms.cite: their cognition arrives with the self-evidence of perception, svam iva, like one's own property, and na atisankate, without residual doubt

axioms.name: grahana-sthana
axioms.cite: Argument gets its grip only where doubt has a foothold, and a cognition that presents itself perceptually offers none
their cognition arrives with the self-evidence of perception, svam iva, like one's own property, and na atisankate, without residual doubt

denials.name: nivartayet
denials.cite: katham anyah nivartayet, how could another dislodge it?

denials.name: sankamanvita
denials.cite: a cognition that presents itself perceptually offers none

claims.name: asrita
claims.cite: and it stands on the side of perception

claims.name: atyanta-nirvikalpa
claims.cite: When someone does not doubt a cognition
a seeing that is, as it were, his own possession

rejected_readings.label: argument_power_universality
rejected_readings.rendering: Rational argument has the power to overturn any cognition, whether it is grounded in perception or not.
rejected_readings.why: Contradicts the core epistemological claim: argument only has grip where doubt already exists. A perceptually-grounded cognition offers no foothold for argument to work.

rejected_readings.label: perception_as_doubt_source
rejected_readings.rendering: Perceptual knowledge is itself subject to doubt, so argument can still be used to overturn perceptually-grounded cognitions.
rejected_readings.why: Denies the phenomenological fact the verse establishes: that a cognition standing on perception's side is presented without residual doubt. This misses the property atyanta-nirvikalpa essential to perceptual cognition.

notes: The verse's epistemology turns on the distinction between cognitions that carry doubt and those that carry self-evidence. The contract encodes the core relational claim: argument has grip only where doubt exists as a foothold; perceptual cognition, by virtue of its self-evidential character, lacks this foothold and is thus immune to argumentative dislodgement.
